intro: Python's indentation-based syntax and duck-typed runtime are a world apart from JavaScript's event-loop model and prototype chain. When porting Python scripts to JavaScript you'll need to map list comprehensions to Array methods like `.map()` and `.filter()`, replace generators with async iterators, and substitute standard-library modules such as `os` and `json` with Node.js equivalents or browser APIs.

diffs.from: Indentation + colon define blocks. No braces required.
diffs.to: Curly braces delimit blocks. Indentation is stylistic only.

diffs.from: Dynamic; PEP 484 type hints are optional and ignored at runtime.
diffs.to: Dynamic; TypeScript superset adds compile-time enforcement.

diffs.from: asyncio event loop; threading for CPU-bound work (GIL applies).
diffs.to: Single-threaded event loop; Promises and async/await are native.

diffs.from: list, dict, set, tuple with built-in list comprehensions.
diffs.to: Array, Object, Map, Set. No tuples; destructuring fills the gap.

diffs.from: None is the single null value. Falsy alongside 0 and "".
diffs.to: Two null values: null (intentional) and undefined (unset).

examples.title: Filter and transform a list
examples.description: Python list comprehensions map directly to JavaScript Array.filter + Array.map chains.
examples.fromCode:
# Filter even numbers, then square them
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]

result = [n ** 2 for n in numbers if n % 2 == 0]
print(result)  # [4, 16, 36, 64]

examples.title: Simple class with a method
examples.description: Python __init__ maps to JS constructor; f-strings become string concatenation or template literals.

tips.title: Comprehensions → Array methods
tips.body: Replace [x for x in items if cond] with items.filter(x => cond).map(x => transform). For dict comprehensions use Object.fromEntries.

tips.title: None / True / False literals
tips.body: Python's None, True, False become null/undefined, true, false. Pay attention to falsy differences: 0 and "" are falsy in both, but undefined only exists in JS.

tips.title: f-strings → template literals
tips.body: f"Hello {name}" becomes the JS template literal with backtick syntax. Escape any literal backticks in your strings.

tips.title: Exception handling
tips.body: Python's except ExceptionType maps to catch (e) — JS has no checked exceptions. Use instanceof for type-narrowing: if (e instanceof TypeError).
